coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur Co-Doped graphene quantum dots decorated CeO2 nanoparticles/ polyaniline: As high efficient hybrid supercapacitor electrode materials
coredata.dc:creator: Oskueyan, Ghasem
Lakouraj, Moslem Mansour
Mahyari, Mojtaba
coredata.dc:description: Recently, hybrid supercapacitor due to high energy/power density and long cycle life has been attractive field for energy researchers. In this work, we demonstrated the decoration of graphene quantum dots on metal oxide/polyaniline to form efficient electrode materials for a hybrid supercapacitor for the first time. Ultrafine N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) were grown on CeO2 nanoparticles via in situ hydrothermal method. Then, PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2 nanocomposites have been prepared by the chemical oxidation polymerization process. The simultaneous incorporation of S and N species in GQDs provides a larger active surface and greatly increases the contact area with the CeO2 in polymer nanocomposite. The as prepared material was analyzed by spectroscopic techniques and electron microscopy analysis. Electrochemical properties of the PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2 as supercapacitor electrodes were investigated by cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy experiments. The maximum specific capacity of 189 C g−1was obtained for PANI/5 wt % N,S-GQDs@CeO2 nanocomposite at current density of 1 A g−1 in comparison with 175 and 115 C g−1 for PANI/10 wt% N,S-GQDs@CeO2 and PANI/5 wt% CeO2 respectively. Value of specific capacity remained at 75% after 1000 cycles under the current density of 1 A g−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots
CeO2 nanoparticle
Polyaniline
Hybrid supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots on CeO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for preparation of PACG.
FTIR spectra of the CeG(a) and PACe (b), PACG5 and PACG10 nanocomposite (c,d).
XRD pattern of the CeG (a) and PACe(b), PACG5,10 composite (c,d).
The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CeO2(a) and CeG(b).
Raman spectra of CeO2 (a) and CeG(b).
SEM image of CeO2 (a) GQDs (b) CeG(c) and FESEM of PACe (d), PACG 5, 10% (e,f).
TEM image of GQDs (a) and N,S-GQDs@CeO2(b)
Cyclic voltammograms of PACG5 and CeG, PACe, PACG 10 at a scan rate of 50 mV. s−1 in 1 M H2SO4(a) and Cyclic voltammograms of PACG5% in different scan rate (b).
Galvanostatic charge–discharge curve at a current density of 1.0 A g−1 of the CeG, PACe, PACG5, and PACG 10.

Specific capacity of the PACG5 in 1.0 M H2SO4 at different current densities of 1–5 A g−1. (a) And the specific capacity changes of PACG5 at a constant current density of 1.0 A g−1 as a function of cycle number with time duration measurement (b).
Nyquist plots of PACe, PACG5 and PACG10 nanocomposite electrodes.
Turning on a green LED with PACG 5 as an electrode and TEMABF4 as an electrolyte.
Comparison of capacity values for various CeO2 nanocomposites in supercapacitor electrodes.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur Co-Doped graphene quantum dots decorated CeO2 nanoparticles/ polyaniline: As high efficient hybrid supercapacitor electrode materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Polymer Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Organic Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, P.O. Box 47416-95447, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Malek-Ashtar University of Technology, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, P.O. Box 16765-3454, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, hybrid supercapacitor due to high energy/power density and long cycle life has been attractive field for energy researchers. In this work, we demonstrated the decoration of graphene quantum dots on metal oxide/polyaniline to form efficient electrode materials for a hybrid supercapacitor for the first time. Ultrafine N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) were grown on CeO2 nanoparticles via in situ hydrothermal method. Then, PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2 nanocomposites have been prepared by the chemical oxidation polymerization process. The simultaneous incorporation of S and N species in GQDs provides a larger active surface and greatly increases the contact area with the CeO2 in polymer nanocomposite. The as prepared material was analyzed by spectroscopic techniques and electron microscopy analysis. Electrochemical properties of the PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2 as supercapacitor electrodes were investigated by cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy experiments. The maximum specific capacity of 189 C g−1was obtained for PANI/5 wt % N,S-GQDs@CeO2 nanocomposite at current density of 1 A g−1 in comparison with 175 and 115 C g−1 for PANI/10 wt% N,S-GQDs@CeO2 and PANI/5 wt% CeO2 respectively. Value of specific capacity remained at 75% after 1000 cycles under the current density of 1 A g−1.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors or electrochemical capacitors (ECs) are electrochemical storage devices that attempt to bridge the gap between capacitors and batteries [1]. In order to have such advantages as high-power density, high charge/discharge rate, and long cycle life ECs have been very interested in the field of electrochemical energy storage [2,3]. The three groups of supercapacitors are: Electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), pseudocapacitors, and hybrid electrochemical capacitors. EDLCs and pseudocapacitors are limited by the stored energy mechanism [4] and power density [5,6]. To overcome these limitations, hybrid supercapacitors, the third generation of supercapacitors, are highly appreciated.

Hybrid supercapacitors are consisted of a composite of either two or three components of conductive polymers, metal oxides and carbon-based materials [7]. High-performance hybrid supercapacitors requirements are fast redox reaction which various metal oxides such as RuO2, IrO2 and MnO2 were used for this purpose. However, the toxic nature, high cost, and resource abundance challenges the use of these materials [8]. Among metal oxides, CeO2 has been proposed to be a promising candidate [9], but poor electrical conductivity and agglomeration of CeO2 nanoparticles are limitations for use in supercapacitor electrode. Combination of CeO2 NPs with conductive carbon-based materials as support can be a solution for these problems [10].

Graphene due to its exclusive properties such as high surface area, high electrical conductivity and high charge retention is a smart choice [11]. For example, we can refer to Wang and Dezfuli,s work for preparation of CeO2/graphene nanocomposite supercapacitor which showed a specific capacitance of 259 C g−1 at the current density of 2 A g−1 [8] and 191 C g−1 at the current density of 3 A g−1 [9].

Despite the benefits of graphene, the tendency for getting high stability, caused to aggregation of layers and leading to low electrical conductivity and effective surface area [12]. To this end, use of stable graphene derivatives such as graphene foam, functionalized graphene sheet, and graphene quantum dots can be an efficient solution.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) had been considered an electrode material in the supercapacitor due to excellent properties, such as high electrical conductivity, high surface area, low toxicity, and excellent dispersion. For example, Mondal et al., introduced a composite of graphene quantum dots and polyaniline which showed high specific capacity value ∼835 C g−1 at current density 1 A g−1 [13].

Polyaniline as a supercapacitor electrode because of highly π-conjugation and reversible doping-dedoping process has some advantages such as high specific capacity and low cost but disadvantages of this compound includes low mechanical stability and cyclic life. There are several procedures for overcome this restriction such as composition with metal oxides and carbon allotropes or thermal crosslinking of Aniline. Moreover, reprotonation of polyaniline for reusability is possible. Cao and coworkers reported that the reprotonation of polyaniline to achieve high conductivity should be carried out with low concentration of HCl for 2 h [14–18].

According to our ongoing work on the use of graphene quantum dots based materials for different applications [19–22], in this research, N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) were grown on CeO2 nanoparticles by in situ hydrothermal method. The structure, morphology and electrochemical properties of nanocomposites have been characterized and identified. Then, to enhance the electrochemical performance, nanocomposite of polyaniline with N,S-GQDs@CeO2 (CeG) was prepared via a chemical polymerization. In the other hand, for investigation of GQDs effect on electrochemical properties, nanocomposite of PANI-CeO2 was synthesized and named as PACe. The preparation process for nanocomposite of polyaniline with N,S-GQDs@CeO2 (PACG) is summarized in Fig. 1 .

Citric acid (+99%) and thiourea were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co. Aniline monomers (99.5%) were purchased from Fluka and purified by vacuum distillation before polymerization. Ammonium persulfate (APS, 98%) as oxidant was obtained from Merck Co. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received from Sigma Aldrich and Merck companies. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

For preparation of Cerium oxide, 10 mL aqueous ammonia (25–28% w/w) was added to 0.1 M CeCl3 (99%) in methanol solution under vigorous stirring. After precipitation, the solid was dried at 293 K for 12 h and calcined at 673 K in air for 4 hand finally CeO2 nanoparticles (CeO2 NPs) were obtained as yellow powder [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots on CeO2
ce:section.ce:para: N,S-GQDs@CeO2 was synthesized by hydrothermal process. Briefly 0.21 g citric acid (1 mmol) and 0.23 g thiourea (3 mmol) were dissolved in 5 mL water and stirred for several time to obtain a clear solution. Then, 172 mg CeO2 (1 mmol) was added to this solution and sonicated for 10 min. This brown solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and placed at 160 °C for 6 h. The final product was collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 5 min and dried in vacuum oven. The obtained product was in the form of gray powders (Fig. 1.) [24].

For preparing PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2, 1.0 g aniline monomer was dispersed in 30 mL 1 M HCl solution under violent stirring. After stirring for 15 min approximately, N,S-GQDs@CeO2 added to aniline then 2.28 g (NH4)2S2O8 (dissolved in10 mL deionized water) was dropped into the above system. The polymerization was completed for 12 h at room temperature. Finally, the product was collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h under vacuum to obtain a dark green PANI nanocomposites [25]. The mass ratios for N,S-GQDs@CeO2to aniline were, 1: 20 and 1: 10, which the corresponding product was abbreviated as PACG5 and PACG10 respectively.

Supercapacitor measurements were performed on a biologic VSP 300 electrochemical workstation in 1 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte solutions under three-electrode assembly with a carbon fiber paper working electrode (WE), Pt foil and Ag/AgCl as counter and reference electrodes respectively. The PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2 composite modified electrode was prepared by mixing 80 wt% of composite powder, 15 wt % of acetylene black, 5 wt % of PVDF, and a few drops of NMP. Then the slurry was pressed onto carbon fiber paper acting as working electrodes and dried in vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h before use. The mass loading of active materials in the electrode was about 1 mg and the size of the working electrode was 0.75 cm2 [26]. The specific capacity was calculated from the chronopotentiometric curves (CV) using Eq. (1) [27].
(1)
Δ Q = C× Δ U
where ΔQ is the charge stored (expressed in coulombs, C, or mAh) and ΔU is the width of the voltage window (V).

Our approach was guided to use the PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2 for hybrid supercapacitor (i) use inexpensive raw material for making supercapacitor electrode (ii) an efficient charge storage properties in high current density (iii) increase the electrical conductivity and electrochemical surface area of CeO2 for supercapacitor applications.

The FT-IR spectra of the samples were shown in Fig. 2 . As seen in Fig. 2a for CeG, the absorption band at 440 cm−1 is corresponds to stretching vibrations of CeO [28]. The peaks at 1000–1250 cm−1 are related to the COH and CC bond vibrations which evidently prove the presence of GQDs on the CeO2 nanoparticles [29]. The band at 1653 cm−1 is assigned to the vibrational absorption band of CONH while the bands at 1429 and 1507 cm−1 are from the bending vibrations of CN and CC respectively [20]. The broad bands about 3100–3400 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of OH and NH [20]. The FTIR spectra of PACG5 nanocomposite are illustrated in Fig. 2c. The main characteristic peaks are as follows: The peaks located at 1475 and 1559 cm−1, which corresponded to the CC stretching and vibration mode of the benzenoid and quinonoid units, can be observed; the peaks at 1300 and 1241 cm−1 are assigned to the CN bending and CN stretching mode of quinonoid unit; the aromatic CH in-plane band at 1107 cm−1; The peaks at 800 and 1047 cm−1 are ascribed to the CCl group, and the CH bonds in 1,4-disubstituted benzene, respectively [30]. The FTIR spectrum of PACe showed in Fig. 2b, is identical to that of PACG.

Typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of cerium oxide, CeG, PACe, PACG5, and PACG10 are shown in Fig. 3 . As can be seen, the XRD patterns of PACGS indicating that the Ceria decorated GQD particles have been well immobilized by PANI. Fig. 3a shows the XRD of CeG that represents a crystalline nanostructure in which the N,S-GQDs were synthesized in 160 °C in presence of CeO2 [31], and all of the diffraction peaks at about 2θ = 31°, 33°, 52°, 64°, 66°,76° indicated the characteristic peaks of Cerium oxide according to JCPDS 78–0694 standard that were barely changed [32], due to the low content of S,N-GQDs in the composites [33].

For PACe, PACG5 and PACG10, the intensity of XRD diffraction peaks corresponding to 2θ = 24° of PANI is decreased, which related to low crystallinity of PANI, and indicate that CeG was incorporated in the polymer matrix [34].

In Fig. 4 , the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CeO2 and CeG composites measured in aqueous solution are represented. The absorption of CeG composites extends to the visible range to 800 nm confirmed that CeO2 nanoparticles coated by N,S-GQDs [33].

Fig. 5 shows Raman spectra of CeO2 and CeG composite. The peak at 466 cm−1 is related to the Raman active mode vibration of CeO2. The Raman-active modes of the N,S-GQDs are two additional D and G peaks which located at 1376 and 1581 cm−1 [33].

According to Fig. 6 , SEM of CeO2, GQDs, and CeG clearly show that synthesis of GQD in the presence of CeO2 has changed the morphology of GQD to a laminated structure. PACe, PACG5, and PACG10 composites were further characterized using FE-SEM techniques. Neat PACe showed spherical polymeric nanoparticles. Whereas, as can be seen in Fig. 6e–f, the SEM micrographs of PACG5 and PACG10 composites display a nanorod structure that can enhance electrical conductivity of the PANI composite [29]. Because in thermal synthesis of N,S-GQDs at 160 °C in presence of CeO2, agglomeration of CeO2 nanoparticles took place which produced the nanorods [31]. These nanostructures have large active surface areas that enhance rate capabilities and capacity of supercapacitors. The high accessible surface areas together with electrical conductivity of GQDs composite in conductive PANI matrix can introduce an efficient hybrid supercapacitor [29].
As evident in Fig. 7 , TEM images confirm that the GQDs were successfully deposited on CeO2 particles.

Electrochemical performance of the CeG, PACe, and PACG nanocomposites were investigated by means of cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge curves in 1 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Cyclic voltammograms of CeG, PACe, PACG5, and PACG10 was measured at 50 mV/s scan rates and presented in Fig. 8 a. Two pairs of redox peaks in cyclic voltammograms are related to charge storage properties and reversible charge-discharge behavior and of PANI. Cyclic voltammogram of PANI is composed of two oxidation waves. The transition of leucoemeraldine to emeraldine salt is the first, and the transition from emeraldine salt to pernigraniline state is second [13,35]. Fig. 8b displays the CV curves of the PACG 5 at different scan rates of 10, 20, 50, and 100 mV/s in 1 M H2SO4 at a potential window from −0.2–0.8 V [36].

Selection of this potential window is done according to the most reports on PANI supercapacitors. Because the energy density is directly related to the square of the cell voltage and a potential window less than 0.6 V would be too low for a supercapacitor [15].

As can be observed from Fig. 9 , the specific capacity of PACG5 (189 C g−1) was significantly higher than that of PACG10 (175 C g−1) and PACe (115 C g−1) at 1 A g−1 current density. Symmetric charge/discharge curve in this shape shows that ideal capacitive behavior of the materials. It is found that the specific capacity of PACG5 is greater than that of either CeG or PACe due to highly conductive pathways generated by N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) in composite, which is consistent with the result obtained from the CV in Fig. 8a [37].

Fig. 10 a shows the specific capacity of PACG5 at various current densities. As seen, the specific capacity of the nanocomposite still can reach to 128 C g−1 at current densities of 5 A g−1. The main reason for decreasing is due to the high discharge rate, which leads to electrolyte ions not being able to enter the internal structure of the electro-active material. The cycle life of PACG5, was conducted at 1 A g−1. As displayed in Fig. 10b after 1000 cycles, the specific capacity of PACG retained up to 75% [38]. For investigation of long term stability of PACG5 as an electrode material for supercapacitor, time duration measurement is shown.
Comparison of capacity values for various CeO2 composite electrodes is summarized in Table 1 .

An informative technique for investigation of the supercapacitive performance of electrode materials is electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). At the high frequency (on the upper right portion) the resistance is commonly known as charge transfer resistance. The low frequency (lower left portion) is related to the Warburg resistance resulting from the frequency dependence of ion diffusion/transport in the electrolyte. The Nyquist impedance plots for PACe, PACG 5, 10 electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 solutions over the frequency range from 0.01 to 100 kHz were shown in Fig. 11 . The slope of straight line showed that PACG 5 has better charge storage capacity that is in good agreement with the CV and CP [35].

To evaluate the performance of PACG5 as supercapacitor electrode material, a pouch cell supercapacitor was fabricated; two 12 cm2 electrodes were assembled with celgard tri-layer membrane as the separator and covered with aluminum plate. By tetramethyl ammonium terrafluoroborate (TEMABF4) as e electrolyte, a green LED (at least 2.5 V) was turned on without the need for a voltage booster( Fig. 12 ).

In this work, N,S co-doped graphene quantum dots decorated CeO2 were successfully prepared via the facility and in-situ hydrothermal method. The nanocomposite of polyaniline and N,S-GQDs@CeO2 was used as an electrode material in hybrid supercapacitor which was more efficient than PANI-CeO2. Nanocomposite of PANI with 5% of N,S-GQDs@CeO2 exhibits a specific capacity of 189 C g−1 at 1 A g−1 current density with a carbon fiber paper. This study showed that N,S-doped graphene quantum dots has a positive effect on the electrochemical properties of CeO2 in polyaniline-cerium oxide nanocomposite. The prepared PACG5 nanocomposite is a good candidate for supercapacitor electrode material because the specific capacity of this nanocomposite still can reach to 68% of initial capacitance even at current density of 5 A g−1. Summing up, increasing active surface and electrical conductivity of CeO2 with GQDs in conductive PANI matrix represent an efficient hybrid supercapacitor composite.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support provided by the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF), Grant No: 96001417, is gratefully acknowledged.
